APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This work squarely addresses a core NLP/ML topic—word and sense representation learning—by introducing sememe-aware extensions to Skip-gram and attention-based sense selection, which is directly relevant to top-tier venues. The methodology (jointly learning sememe, sense and word embeddings using HowNet), the proposed models (SSA, SAC, SAT), and standard evaluations (word similarity, analogy, WSD metrics) match the methods and evaluation standards expected. The paper is aimed at the right audience (researchers in semantic representation and word/sense embedding) and is positioned appropriately within the NLP/ML research community.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable novel direction by, for the first time (per the authors), jointly learning sememe, sense and word embeddings using HowNet and by proposing three concrete sememe-encoded extensions of Skip-gram (SSA, SAC, SAT) with attention mechanisms. This clearly differentiates it from prior work that either ignored sememes or did not jointly model all three levels. However, the core problem (word representation learning) is well-studied and the methods are extensions of existing Skip-gram/attention ideas rather than a wholly new paradigm, and the approach omits sememe relational structure, so the contribution is solidly novel but not radical.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—enriching word embeddings with fine-grained semantic units—by, for the first time (per the summary), jointly learning sememe, sense and word representations from HowNet and proposing three sememe-encoded Skip-gram variants (SSA, SAC, SAT). Empirical gains on word similarity, analogy and improved word sense disambiguation indicate the approach could change how researchers integrate lexical/knowledge resources and attention-based sense selection into representation learning. However, reliance on HowNet and the omission of internal sememe relational structure constrain generality, so the work is likely to have strong impact within lexical semantics and resource-rich languages but is not fully transformative across all NLP subfields.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents multiple sememe-encoded models (SSA, SAC, SAT), compares them against reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST) and reports standard metrics (Spearman, Accuracy, Mean Rank) with hyperparameters given, which together provide solid empirical support for the main claims. It also explicitly states limitations (ignoring internal sememe structure) and claims improvements on word similarity/analogy and WSD, and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor gaps remain: only the Chinese Word Analogy dataset is mentioned in the summary (raising questions about dataset diversity/generalization), code is not reported, and it is unclear whether detailed sensitivity analyses or systematic ablations beyond proposing three variant models were performed.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments reporting significant improvements on word similarity and analogy tasks using three sememe-augmented Skip-gram variants, and the paper jointly models sememes, senses and words as claimed. The mathematical approach is a straightforward extension of Skip-gram with attention/aggregation over sememe embeddings and no derivation errors are indicated in the summary. Experimental methodology is broadly sound — reasonable baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, MST), relevant dataset (Chinese analogy), and metrics were used and hyperparameters reported — but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing. A known limitation is acknowledged (ignoring sememe relational structure and reliance on HowNet), which is an unsupported assumption but stated; these are minor issues that do not overturn the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against standard and relevant baselines (Skip-gram, CBOW, GloVe, and MST) and evaluates on established metrics (Spearman, Accuracy, Mean Rank) with hyperparameters reported, suggesting generally fair experimental conditions. It clearly analyzes differences by proposing three sememe-encoded strategies (SSA, SAC, SAT) and attributing gains to sememe attention, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not indicate the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, and evaluation appears limited to the Chinese Word Analogy dataset, so the comparisons are strong but have minor omissions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow from motivation through methodology (three clear strategies: SSA, SAC, SAT) to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (joint learning of sememes, senses, and words using HowNet) are stated early. Writing quality is reported as good, related work coverage is thorough, and experimental details such as datasets and hyperparameters are provided, which supports reproducibility. However, the summary does not confirm consistency of notation or the effectiveness of figures/tables/examples, so minor presentation issues may remain.

main_claims: For the first time, jointly learns representations of sememes, senses and words using HowNet annotations.
Proposes three sememe-encoded models (SSA, SAC, SAT) that extend Skip-gram with sememe information and attention mechanisms for sense selection.
Demonstrates that incorporating sememes via the attention scheme improves word representation learning, with models significantly outperforming baselines on word similarity and word analogy tasks.
Unified sememe-aware representations provide more explicit explanations of both word and sense embeddings and enable improved word sense disambiguation.
_summary.methodology: Extend the Skip-gram framework to jointly learn sememe, sense and word embeddings using sememe annotations from HowNet as semantic regularization; three strategies are proposed (Simple Sememe Aggregation, Sememe Attention over Context, Sememe Attention over Target) that use averaging and attention over sememe/sense embeddings to build word representations.
limitations: For simplicity, the internal relational/hierarchical structure among sememes is ignored: each sense's sememes are treated as an unordered set.
Relies on the assumption (from HowNet) that a limited set of annotated sememes can well represent senses and words.
